narrative: Dear [Student's Name], I know this rejection letter stings—dreaming of law school only to have your application turned away because our predictive model sees a 63% chance you'll struggle with the bar exam feels deeply unfair, and I'm truly sorry it's landed this way for you. Picture the model as an experienced advisor piecing together your story from the patterns it's learned from thousands of students like you, and what stood out most was your Year 3 decile of 3, which placed you in the lower third of your peers and whispered strongly of the challenges ahead in mastering the intense demands of legal exams. Right behind that was your Year 1 decile of 5, showing that even early on, your grades didn't climb as high as those who typically power through to bar success. Layered on top was your middle family income bracket, which the model associates with fewer resources for the grueling prep that often tips the scales. Your LSAT score of 25 didn't help either, signaling to the model a tougher road in tackling the bar's complex reasoning under pressure. As an Asian male going full-time, those traits combined with your undergraduate GPA of 2.4 to paint a picture of someone who's resilient but facing headwinds that have tripped up others in similar spots before. It's not that any one thing doomed you—it's this whole mosaic that made the model cautious, predicting failure more likely than not. You've got target_law set at 1, showing your heart's in it, and that's powerful. The good news? This isn't destiny; many students beat these odds by targeting weak spots like boosting study strategies or seeking extra support. Law schools want predictors like this to protect their pass rates, but your story isn't over—you can rewrite it starting now. Hang in there; you've got the drive, and with focused effort, the bar could still be yours.
